name: Vía Verde de la Jara Loop
name_original: Pasando por el río Tajo en la Vía Verde de la Jara – circular desde Belvís de la Jara
description: The Vía Verde de la Jara Loop is a 23.9-mile moderate touring cycling route, currently closed due to safety concerns.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Belvís De La Jara traverse a landscape characterized by gentle hills and Mediterranean vegetation, shaped by the Tajo and Gévalo rivers. The region features extensive olive and holm oak groves, contributing to its scenic beauty. Natural lagoons and river valleys offer diverse environments for cycling. The area's proximity to the Montes de Toledo provides varied terrain for outdoor exploration. Best touring cycling routes around Belvís De La Jara The most popular touring cycling route is Vía Verde de la Jara Loop, a 23.9 miles (38.5 km) trail that takes 2 hours 27 minutes to complete. This moderate route follows a former railway line, offering a generally flat and scenic path. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Puente Azután Viewpoint – Huso River Valley and Tunnel loop from Calera-Chozas, a difficult 51.2 miles (82.4 km) path. This route explores river valleys and includes significant elevation changes. Local touring cyclists also love the Cane Tunnel – Puente Azután Viewpoint loop from Calera-Chozas, a 22.7 miles (36.6 km) trail leading through Mediterranean landscapes, often completed in about 2 hours 16 minutes. Touring cycling around Belvís De La Jara is defined by gentle hills, river valleys, and olive groves. The network offers options for both moderate and more challenging cycling experiences. The routes in Belvís De La Jara are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 100 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Belvís De La Jara's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Belvís De La Jara?
A: There are 15 touring cycling routes around Belvís De La Jara, offering a variety of experiences for cyclists. These routes are rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars.

Q: What are the difficulty levels of touring cycling routes in Belvís De La Jara?
A: The routes around Belvís De La Jara cater to various fitness levels. You'll find 8 moderate routes and 7 difficult routes, ensuring options for both experienced cyclists and those looking for a challenge. There are no easy routes specifically for touring cycling listed in this guide.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Belvís De La Jara?
A: The region's Mediterranean climate makes spring and autumn particularly pleasant for touring cycling. During these seasons, the natural lagoons are especially beautiful, and the weather is generally mild, ideal for exploring the gentle hills and olive groves.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Belvís De La Jara?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes in the area are circular. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31434299" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cane Tunnel – Puente Azután Viewpoint loop from Calera-Chozas&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 22.7-mile route, and the more challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31436353" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente Azután Viewpoint – Huso River Valley and Tunnel loop from Calera-Chozas&lt;/a&gt; covers 51.2 miles.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: The routes traverse a landscape rich in natural beauty. You can expect to see extensive olive and holm oak groves, gentle hills, and river valleys shaped by the Tajo and Gévalo rivers. Notable landmarks include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5417642" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente Azután Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3853975" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Huso River Valley and Tunnel&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features natural lagoons, particularly scenic in spring and autumn.

Q: Is the Vía Verde de la Jara accessible for touring cyclists?
A: While the Vía Verde de la Jara is a significant feature of the region, the specific 'Vía Verde de la Jara Loop' covering 23.9 miles is currently closed due to safety concerns. However, other routes in the broader La Jara area, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38209160" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente Azután Viewpoint – Vía Verde de la Jara loop from La Nava de Ricomalillo&lt;/a&gt;, incorporate sections or views related to the Vía Verde, offering a similar experience.

Q: Are there any cultural or historical sites accessible by touring bike?
A: Yes, the area offers cultural detours. The archaeological site of Ciudad de Vascos provides a glimpse into ancient history. Additionally, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20140143" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Talavera de la Reina – Zamora Gate and Jail loop from Calera-Chozas&lt;/a&gt; can lead you to nearby towns with historical significance, such as Talavera de la Reina, where you can explore its gates and jail.

Q: What kind of terrain should I expect on touring cycling routes in Belvís De La Jara?
A: The terrain is characterized by gentle hills, river valleys, and traditional agricultural landscapes with olive and holm oak groves. You'll encounter a mix of paved roads and dirt tracks, offering a tranquil setting for connecting with nature and rural life. The proximity to the Montes de Toledo also contributes to varied terrain.

Q: Are there places to eat or stay along the routes?
A: Belvís de la Jara and surrounding villages offer cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options. While specific establishments are not listed in the route details, planning your stops in towns like Calera-Chozas or Alcaudete de la Jara, or even Belvís de la Jara itself, will provide opportunities for refreshments and overnight stays.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Belvís De La Jara?
A: The touring cycling routes in Belvís De La Jara are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the scenic beauty, the quiet Mediterranean landscapes, and the varied terrain that offers both moderate and challenging experiences.

Q: Is there public transport available for cyclists with bikes in Belvís De La Jara?
A: Public transport options for cyclists with bikes in rural areas like Belvís De La Jara can be limited. It's advisable to check local bus services directly for their bicycle policies and availability, as dedicated bike-friendly transport might not be widely offered. Most cyclists prefer to drive to a starting point with their bikes.

Q: Where can I find parking for my car when starting a touring cycling route?
A: Parking is generally available in the towns and villages that serve as starting points for these routes, such as Belvís de la Jara, Calera-Chozas, or La Nava de Ricomalillo. Look for public parking areas within these settlements, which are typically well-suited for leaving your vehicle while you cycle.
tips: Alcaudete de la Jara is in a deep plain, surrounded by hills of medium elevation. It is known as "Levante de Castilla" thanks to its extraordinary microclimate, which makes it enjoy very mild temperatures throughout the year.
The view of the Azután Viaduct from the Amador Bridge, in the La Jara region (Toledo), is one of the most spectacular along the Tajo Nature Trail GR-113 and the La Jara Green Route.

The view of the Tagus River from the Azután Bridge, located in Aldeanueva de Barbarroya (Toledo), is one of the most peaceful and natural views in the La Jara region. This bridge offers a privileged panoramic view of the river in a sparsely built environment, ideal for those seeking serene landscapes and nature trails.

Aldeanueva de Barbarroya, in the La Jara region (Toledo), is a small town with a wealth of natural, historical, and cultural treasures. It is especially known for its location on the La Jara Greenway and for being a stopover point on the GR-113 Tajo Nature Trail.

"Belvís" means "beautiful view" perhaps referring to the beautiful landscape that dominates from its hills. The most interesting building is the Iglesia Parroquial de San Andrés Apostol, in Mudejar style.

faqs.question: What is the current status of the Vía Verde de la Jara Loop?
faqs.answer: Please be aware that the Vía Verde de la Jara is temporarily closed due to significant safety concerns. Authorities have warned against using the trail, citing poor pavement conditions, lack of lighting in tunnels, and landslides as major hazards. It is advised to avoid attempting to cycle this route until official notification of its reopening.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the Vía Verde de la Jara Loop?
faqs.answer: Before its temporary closure, the Vía Verde de la Jara was known for its generally flat and easy terrain, built on a disused railway line. The surface is a mix of asphalt, tamped gravel, and sometimes dirt track, making it suitable for mountain bikes or gravel bikes. While there are minimal elevation changes, the route is largely accessible with gentle gradients.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the Vía Verde de la Jara Loop?
faqs.answer: When the trail is open, you can experience a landscape of austere beauty, characterized by granite and slate formations, Mediterranean forest, and dehesas. Notable features include impressive railway infrastructure with six viaducts and eighteen tunnels. Highlights on this specific loop include the Alcaudete de la Jara Town Hall and Church, the Amador Bridge or Azután Viaduct, and the Puente Azután Viewpoint. The route also passes through Aldeanueva de Barbarroya and near the Parish Church of San Andrés Apóstol in Belvís de la Jara.

faqs.question: Is the Vía Verde de la Jara Loop suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Historically, yes, the Vía Verde de la Jara was considered an excellent option for touring cycling, including for beginners, due to its generally flat and easy nature with hardly any inclines. However, please remember the trail is currently closed due to safety concerns, making it unsuitable for anyone until it officially reopens.

faqs.question: Does the Vía Verde de la Jara Loop intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this loop overlap with other official trails. You will find it intersects with parts of the Camino Natural del Tajo, Camino de la Jara - Etapa 1, and Camino de la Jara - Etapa 2, among others.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Vía Verde de la Jara Loop?
faqs.answer: Before its temporary closure, early spring was often recommended due to the 'endless fields of flowers' that give the 'Jara' (rock rose) its name. The trail also offers picturesque views near the Tagus river and Azután reservoir. However, please note the trail is currently closed and should not be used.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the Vía Verde de la Jara Loop?
faqs.answer: While specific regulations for dogs on the Vía Verde de la Jara are not detailed, generally, dogs are permitted on Vías Verdes in Spain, often requiring them to be on a leash. However, given the trail's current temporary closure due to safety concerns, it is not advisable to bring dogs or attempt to use the route at all.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee to use the Vía Verde de la Jara Loop?
faqs.answer: No, typically Vías Verdes in Spain, including the Vía Verde de la Jara, do not require permits or charge entrance fees for public access. However, please remember that the trail is currently closed due to safety concerns, and access is not permitted.

faqs.question: Where does the Vía Verde de la Jara Loop start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: This specific loop starts and ends in Belvís de la Jara. While parking availability in Belvís de la Jara is generally good, it's important to reiterate that the Vía Verde de la Jara is currently closed due to safety concerns, and access is not recommended.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or accommodation near the Vía Verde de la Jara Loop?
faqs.answer: Before its temporary closure, the route passed through towns like Aldeanueva de Barbarroya, which would offer basic services. The starting point, Belvís de la Jara, also has local amenities. Historically, the visitor center at Campillo-Sevilleja station also provided facilities. However, with the trail currently closed, the availability and accessibility of these services for trail users are impacted.

faqs.question: What should I be aware of regarding tunnels on the Vía Verde de la Jara Loop?
faqs.answer: The Vía Verde de la Jara features eighteen tunnels, with one extending for an entire kilometer. Before the trail's temporary closure, not all tunnels were artificially lit, meaning a flashlight was essential for safe passage. Given the current safety concerns, including lack of lighting, these tunnels pose a significant hazard, and the trail should not be used.
